_meta.authoringNotes: Medical content reviewed against published parasitology literature on Ancylostoma and Necator hookworm epidemiology, clinical presentation, treatment protocols, and iron deficiency anemia from hookworm.

h1Title: Ancylostomiasis (Hookworm Infection): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Ancylostomiasis is a soil-transmitted helminthiasis caused by hookworms — Ancylostoma duodenale and Necator americanus — that attach to the intestinal mucosa and feed on blood, causing chronic iron deficiency anemia in their human hosts. An estimated 400-600 million people are infected globally, making hookworm one of the most common parasitic infections worldwide, with the greatest burden in tropical and subtropical regions of sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and Latin America. Eggs are deposited in soil via human feces, where they hatch into larvae that penetrate the skin of individuals walking barefoot — the classic transmission route in endemic rural communities. Each hookworm feeds on approximately 0.03-0.2 mL of blood per day; heavy infections (100-500 worms) can cause blood losses of 20-100 mL daily, rapidly depleting iron stores and producing iron deficiency anemia. Albendazole (single 400 mg dose) or pyrantel pamoate are the first-line treatments, and reinfection is prevented by mass drug administration and improved sanitation.

keyStats.prevalence: An estimated 400-600 million people infected with hookworm globally; A. duodenale and N. americanus are the two major species; hookworm is one of the three most prevalent soil-transmitted helminths (along with Ascaris lumbricoides and Trichuris trichiura)
keyStats.demographics: Hookworm infection disproportionately affects the poorest populations in tropical regions — agricultural workers, children, and women of reproductive age bear the highest burden; heavy infections causing anemia are most common in children aged 5-14 and in pregnant women
keyStats.avgAge: Peak intensity of infection occurs in children and adolescents aged 5-20 years; clinical anemia typically manifests in this age group and in women of reproductive age
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 4-5 billion people are at risk globally; 300-500 million have clinical hookworm disease; WHO estimates that hookworm causes 2-3 million disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) lost annually

definition: Ancylostomiasis (ICD-10: B76.0) is a chronic parasitic infection of the small intestine caused by two species of hookworm: Ancylostoma duodenale and Necator americanus. Adult worms attach to the villous mucosa of the duodenum and proximal jejunum using a buccal capsule with cutting plates (A. duodenale) or a semi-lunar cutting plate (N. americanus), and feed on blood. The resulting daily blood loss per worm (0.03-0.2 mL/day for A. duodenale, 0.01-0.03 mL/day for N. americanus) can accumulate to significant losses in heavy infections, causing chronic iron deficiency anemia. A. duodenale can also be acquired orally (not just through skin penetration), and can cause transient respiratory symptoms (often called Wakana syndromes) from larval migration through the lungs. The life cycle involves skin penetration by infective larvae, larval migration through the lungs (causing Löffler syndrome), expectoration and swallowing, maturation to adult worms in the small intestine, and egg production within 5-10 weeks of infection.

typesClassification.description: Light hookworm infections (fewer than 20-30 worms) in well-nourished individuals may cause no clinical symptoms. Iron stores are adequate to compensate for the small daily blood loss. These individuals are still shedding eggs in stool, contributing to environmental transmission.

Moderate infections (30-100 worms) with mild-moderate daily blood loss causing gradual iron depletion over months to years. Patients develop iron deficiency without overt anemia — low ferritin, low serum iron, normal or elevated TIBC. More common in populations with marginal iron intake.

Heavy infections (more than 100 worms, often 100-500) with significant daily blood loss causing frank iron deficiency anemia — microcytic hypochromic anemia with low ferritin, low serum iron, and elevated TIBC. The degree of anemia correlates with worm burden and underlying iron nutrition.

A distinct clinical entity from A. duodenale — caused by the oral route of infection (ingestion of larvae), leading to abdominal pain, nausea, anorexia, and diarrhea within 2-4 weeks of infection, and in some cases, a syndrome of cough, pharyngitis, and wheezing from larval pulmonary migration. This is distinct from the typical chronic hookworm presentation.

typesClassification.type: Cutaneous larva migrans (Ancylostoma braziliense or caninum hookworm of dogs/cats)
typesClassification.description: Although caused by animal hookworms (not the human A. duodenale or N. americanus), cutaneous larva migrans (CLM) is clinically relevant — it presents as serpiginous, pruritic, erythematous skin tracks caused by larvae that migrate in the superficial epidermis (cannot penetrate deeper in non-permissive hosts). CLM is acquired from dog/cat hookworm larvae in sand or soil and is common in travelers to tropical beaches.

primarySymptoms: Fatigue, weakness, and exercise intolerance — the most common presenting symptom of hookworm anemia; reduced hemoglobin impairs tissue oxygen delivery, causing disproportionate fatigue on exertion and reduced work capacity in adults.

Pallor (noticeably pale skin, conjunctivae, and nail beds) — reflects reduced hemoglobin concentration; pallor may develop insidiously over months and may be attributed to other causes in endemic areas where anemia is common.

Dyspnea on exertion and reduced exercise tolerance — as iron deficiency anemia worsens, patients develop dyspnea with activities that were previously well-tolerated (climbing stairs, walking to school, carrying loads).
Dizziness, lightheadedness, and headaches — particularly upon standing (orthostatic dizziness) from reduced blood viscosity and impaired oxygen delivery to the brain.

Epigastric or periumbilical discomfort, nausea, and anorexia — adult hookworms attached to the duodenal mucosa cause local inflammation; epigastric pain can mimic peptic ulcer disease; nausea and reduced appetite compound poor iron intake.
Diarrhea or irregular bowel movements — mild malabsorption and local intestinal inflammation from worm attachment can cause loose stools or alternating constipation and diarrhea.
Periorbital puffiness and pedal edema in severe anemia — hypoalbuminemia from chronic disease and malnutrition causes peripheral edema; when combined with anemia, patients can develop anasarca.

Pica (appetite for non-nutritive substances) — an unusual appetite for clay, soil, or ice (pagophagia) is a characteristic symptom of iron deficiency and is common in hookworm-infected children in endemic areas.
earlyWarningSigns: Pallor, fatigue, and pica in a child or adult living in or returning from a tropical endemic region — the combination of pallor + fatigue + pica is highly suggestive of iron deficiency from hookworm

Low hemoglobin on routine blood count in someone from an endemic tropical region — iron deficiency anemia in a tropical setting should trigger stool examination for hookworm eggs before assuming nutritional or dietary etiology

Unexplained iron deficiency anemia in an adult male or post-menopausal woman — hookworm should be considered as a cause of iron deficiency in any person with exposure to endemic regions, regardless of apparent dietary adequacy

Pruritic papular eruption on the feet after barefoot walking in endemic areas — the initial skin penetration site (ground itch) appears within hours to days of larval skin penetration and is an early clinical clue to hookworm acquisition
Persistent eosinophilia with iron deficiency anemia — helminth infections including hookworm typically cause mild peripheral eosinophilia (usually 500-1,500 cells/mcL)
emergencySigns: Severe anemia (hemoglobin below 6-7 g/dL) with cardiac decompensation — in patients with longstanding heavy hookworm infection, severe iron deficiency anemia can cause high-output cardiac failure with tachycardia, cardiomegaly, and respiratory distress

Acute upper GI bleeding — heavy hookworm burden can cause significant blood loss from the small intestinal mucosa; melena (black tarry stools) or hematemesis (vomiting blood) can occur but are less common than in other causes of upper GI bleeding

Severe protein-calorie malnutrition in a child with chronic hookworm infection — chronic anemia and malabsorption compound malnutrition in children already on marginal diets; Kwashiorkor or marasmus can develop
Worm bolus obstruction — rare but documented in very heavy infections (thousands of worms); causes small bowel obstruction presenting with vomiting, distension, and colicky abdominal pain
Severe eosinophilia with pulmonary symptoms — heavy hookworm infection or hyperinfection can cause marked eosinophilia and Löffler syndrome (transient pulmonary infiltrates with dyspnea and cough)

causes.cause: Skin penetration by hookworm larvae (Ancylostoma duodenale or Necator americanus)
causes.description: The primary route of human infection — infective filariform larvae in contaminated soil penetrate the skin of an individual walking barefoot or with exposed feet. The larvae enter the bloodstream, migrate to the lungs, penetrate the alveolar capillaries, ascend the bronchial tree, are expectorated, swallowed, and mature into adult worms in the small intestine within 5-10 weeks. This is the classic transmission route for N. americanus and the predominant route for A. duodenale in agricultural settings.

causes.cause: Oral ingestion of Ancylostoma duodenale larvae
causes.description: A. duodenale larvae can also be ingested directly (not requiring skin penetration), particularly via contaminated food or water. This route is important for A. duodenale and can cause a more rapid onset (Wakana syndromes) with pulmonary and GI symptoms within 2-4 weeks. Ingestion is thought to be the predominant route of A. duodenale infection in some endemic communities where food contamination is common.

causes.cause: Contact with contaminated soil (farming, mining, defecating in fields)
causes.description: Defecating in open fields (environmental contamination with human feces) and walking barefoot on contaminated agricultural land are the dominant transmission dynamics in endemic communities. Farming in rice paddies, cultivating sugarcane, and working in mines (where larvae thrive in warm, moist soil) are high-risk occupations.

causes.cause: Lack of sanitation and inadequate sewage disposal
causes.description: Open defecation is the root cause of soil-transmitted helminth transmission — hookworm eggs in human feces deposited on soil hatch into infective larvae. Communities without latrines or sewage treatment have high environmental contamination and transmission rates. Mass drug administration and improved sanitation are the primary interventions.

causes.cause: Climate and soil conditions favoring larval survival
causes.description: Hookworm larvae require warm, moist, shaded soil to survive — they migrate to the soil surface in humid conditions and ascend plant stems and grass blades, maximizing contact with human skin. Sandy loam soils with adequate organic matter provide the ideal environment. The transmission season corresponds to the rainy season in most tropical regions.

riskFactors.factor: Barefoot walking in endemic tropical regions
riskFactors.description: The single most significant behavioral risk factor for hookworm acquisition — any barefoot contact with contaminated soil in endemic areas (walking to school, farming, defecating outdoors) allows larval skin penetration. Shoes or sandals virtually eliminate hookworm transmission.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or occupation in tropical endemic areas
riskFactors.description: Rural farming communities in tropical and subtropical regions of sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, Latin America, and the Caribbean have the highest hookworm prevalence. Sugarcane cutters, rice farmers, and tea pickers have particularly high exposure.

riskFactors.factor: Poor sanitation (open defecation, lack of latrines)
riskFactors.description: The absence of latrines or toilet facilities allows human feces to contaminate the soil with hookworm eggs. Open defecation is the fundamental driver of soil-transmitted helminth transmission and the root cause of endemic hookworm disease.

Children in endemic areas have the highest intensity of hookworm infection — they play barefoot in contaminated soil, have lower immunity due to less prior exposure, and have higher per-worm blood loss relative to body weight than adults.

Women aged 15-45 years have elevated iron requirements (menstruation, pregnancy, lactation) that compound the blood loss from hookworm. Hookworm anemia in pregnancy is associated with low birth weight and maternal mortality in high-burden areas.

Inadequate dietary iron intake (from limited meat, fish, or iron-fortified foods) accelerates the development of iron deficiency anemia once hookworm infection is acquired. Populations with iron-rich diets can tolerate light hookworm infections without developing anemia.

Immunocompromised individuals may develop more severe hookworm infections and anemia; Strongyloides co-infection is common in hookworm-endemic areas and can cause hyperinfection syndrome in immunocompromised hosts.

affectedDemographics: Hookworm infection affects 400-600 million people globally — the highest prevalence is in sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, Southeast Asia, and Latin America; prevalence in rural endemic communities can reach 50-80%

Children aged 5-14 years and women of reproductive age bear the greatest burden of hookworm anemia — children have the highest worm burdens, and women have increased iron requirements from menstruation and pregnancy
Agricultural workers and subsistence farmers in tropical regions represent the most heavily exposed occupational group — farming barefoot in endemic fields is the dominant transmission pathway
Tourists and travelers to endemic areas who walk barefoot on beaches or in rural areas are at risk for acquiring hookworm — CLM is more common than intestinal hookworm in travelers

The burden of hookworm disease is overwhelmingly concentrated in the world's poorest populations — there is a direct correlation between hookworm prevalence and poverty, lack of sanitation, and limited access to healthcare
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of hookworm infection is established by identifying hookworm eggs in stool using microscopy. A formalin-ethyl acetate concentration technique or the Kato-Katz thick-smear method is used — the characteristic hookworm egg is oval, approximately 60 micrometers x 40 micrometers, with a thin shell and two to eight cleavage cells. Concentration techniques increase sensitivity for light infections. Stool examination is repeated on three separate occasions (each on a different day) because egg output is intermittent — a single negative stool exam does not exclude hookworm infection. The Kato-Katz method also provides a semi-quantitative egg count that correlates with worm burden — guiding treatment intensity. Serology (ELISA for hookworm antigens) can detect infection before eggs appear but is not routinely available in endemic settings. Peripheral eosinophilia is typically mild (500-1,500 cells/mcL). Hemoglobin, serum iron, ferritin, and TIBC should be measured to assess the degree of iron deficiency anemia. The combination of low serum iron, low ferritin, and elevated TIBC confirms iron deficiency anemia as the cause of the anemia. Colonoscopy or capsule endoscopy in patients with unexplained iron deficiency and negative stool exams may reveal adult worms or blood ooze from the small intestinal mucosa.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool microscopy for hookworm eggs (Kato-Katz or concentration technique)
diagnosticTests.purpose: The definitive diagnostic test for hookworm infection — identifies hookworm eggs in stool using microscopy. The Kato-Katz method provides semi-quantitative egg counts (eggs per gram of stool) that correlate with worm burden. Three stool samples on different days are required to maximize sensitivity because egg output is intermittent.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fresh stool sample collected in the morning (when eggs are most concentrated); examined by microscopy using Kato-Katz thick-smear (results within 1-2 hours) or concentration technique. Egg count provides an estimate of worm burden.

diagnosticTests.test: Hemoglobin, serum iron, ferritin, and TIBC
diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines the degree of iron deficiency — hemoglobin quantifies anemia severity; serum ferritin (the most specific test for iron deficiency when low) reflects iron stores; serum iron and TIBC confirm iron deficiency pattern (low iron, elevated TIBC, low transferrin saturation). Ferritin below 15 ng/mL confirms iron deficiency in the absence of inflammation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; hemoglobin available within 1 hour; iron studies from reference lab in 1-3 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Mild peripheral eosinophilia (500-1,500 cells/mcL) is a typical finding in hookworm infection and other soil-transmitted helminths. Eosinophilia is most prominent during the larval migration phase (2-4 weeks after skin penetration) and may normalize in chronic intestinal infection. Absence of eosinophilia does not exclude hookworm.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard CBC with differential; results within 1 hour.

diagnosticTests.test: Duodenal aspirate (for adult worms on endoscopy)
diagnosticTests.purpose: In patients with unexplained iron deficiency anemia and negative stool examinations (light infections may be below the detection threshold), upper GI endoscopy with duodenal aspirate or video capsule endoscopy may visualize adult hookworms or mucosal damage.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Upper GI endoscopy (EGD) under sedation; duodenal aspirate examined for adult worms; results available during the procedure.

diagnosticTests.test: Chest X-ray during larval migration (Löffler syndrome)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Transient pulmonary infiltrates (fleeting, migratory infiltrates on sequential X-rays) on chest imaging during the larval migration phase (2-4 weeks after acquisition) indicates Löffler syndrome from hookworm and other STH larval migration. Most hookworm infections do not have significant pulmonary involvement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard chest X-ray; results available immediately.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool culture for larval identification (Harada-Mori filter paper method)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Culture of stool on filter paper or in charcoal culture allows hookworm larvae to hatch and develop — larval morphology distinguishes Ancylostoma (rhabditiform larvae with short buccal capsule) from Necator (longer buccal capsule). Used in reference parasitology labs for species identification.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fresh stool sample sent to reference parasitology lab; results in 3-5 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of hookworm infection (ancylostomiasis) is primarily antihelmintic and depends on infection severity and patient clinical status. The first-line agent is albendazole 400 mg as a single oral dose, which achieves cure rates above 80 percent and egg reduction rates above 90 percent. An alternative is mebendazole 100 mg orally twice daily for 3 days, with comparable efficacy. Pyrantel pamoate is another option, particularly in pregnancy, though data are more limited. Iron supplementation is prescribed concurrently for patients with iron deficiency anemia from chronic blood loss, typically oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg three times daily until hemoglobin normalizes and iron stores are repleted. Nutritional support with protein and folate supplementation aids recovery. In heavy infection with severe anemia, blood transfusion may be required. Sanitation and hygiene measures are essential: wearing shoes in endemic areas prevents new infection. Deworming of household contacts and regular mass drug administration in endemic communities reduces transmission. Albendazole resistance has been reported in some regions; in treatment failure, mebendazole or pyrantel pamoate can be used as alternatives. Follow-up stool examination at 1 month and again at 3 months confirms cure. Pregnant women in the second and third trimesters can receive pyrantel pamoate, which has a favorable safety profile in pregnancy. Patient education about treatment adherence, monitoring for complications, and recognizing signs of disease progression is essential. Shared decision-making between the patient and treatment team improves outcomes and quality of life. Regular follow-up visits allow assessment of treatment response, management of adverse effects, and adjustment of therapy as needed. Multidisciplinary involvement including specialist physicians, nursing staff, social workers, and mental health professionals optimizes comprehensive care delivery.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole 400 mg orally as a single dose
medicalTreatments.description: The first-line anthelmintic for hookworm — albendazole causes paralysis and expulsion of adult worms by inhibiting microtubule polymerization in the parasite. A single 400 mg dose achieves cure rates of 90-95% for both Ancylostoma and Necator. Side effects include mild abdominal discomfort and, occasionally, diarrhea. Albendazole is safe in children over age 1 year (dose-adjusted for weight).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of 90-95% for hookworm with single-dose albendazole; egg reduction rates of 95-99%; reinfection requires re-treatment. WHO includes albendazole in its recommended MDA program for STH.

medicalTreatments.name: Mebendazole 500 mg orally as a single dose
medicalTreatments.description: An equally effective alternative first-line anthelmintic for hookworm. Mebendazole 500 mg orally as a single dose achieves cure rates equivalent to albendazole (90-95%). Side effects are similar (mild abdominal discomfort). Also safe in children over age 2 years.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable cure rates to albendazole for hookworm; widely used in MDA programs and clinical settings.

medicalTreatments.name: Pyrantel pamoate 10 mg/kg orally for 3 days
medicalTreatments.description: A depolarizing neuromuscular blocking agent that causes spastic paralysis of the worm — effective for both Ancylostoma and Necator. The 3-day course is used particularly in communities where albendazole resistance is suspected or in severe infections requiring more certain cure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: High cure rates with 3-day regimen (approximately 90-95%); useful when first-line drugs are contraindicated or ineffective.

medicalTreatments.name: Ferrous sulfate (or ferrous fumarate) oral iron 60-120 mg elemental iron daily
medicalTreatments.description: Oral iron supplementation is essential for correcting iron deficiency anemia in hookworm patients. Ferrous sulfate 325 mg provides 65 mg elemental iron per tablet; one to two tablets daily is standard. Iron is continued for 3-6 months after hemoglobin normalizes to replenish iron stores (ferritin above 30 ng/mL). Oral iron should be taken on an empty stomach with vitamin C (ascorbic acid 200 mg) to enhance absorption.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Hemoglobin rises by 1-2 g/dL per month of iron therapy; full correction of iron deficiency anemia takes 3-6 months; iron stores (ferritin) take longer to replete (6-12 months).

medicalTreatments.name: IV iron (for severe anemia when oral iron is not tolerated or absorbed)
medicalTreatments.description: IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose or iron sucrose) is used when iron deficiency anemia is severe (hemoglobin below 7 g/dL), oral iron is not tolerated (GI side effects), or absorption is impaired. IV iron bypasses the GI tract and rapidly replenishes iron stores. Useful in pregnant women with severe hookworm anemia who cannot tolerate oral iron.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IV iron rapidly restores iron stores when oral iron is insufficient or not tolerated; Hb improvement detectable within 2-4 weeks of infusion.

surgicalOptions.name: N/A (medical management only)
surgicalOptions.description: Hookworm infection is treated entirely medically with anthelmintic drugs and iron supplementation. Surgical intervention is not part of standard hookworm management. Surgical options are not relevant to this parasitic infection.

alternativeTreatments.name: Levamisole 2.5 mg/kg orally for 3 days
alternativeTreatments.description: An alternative anthelmintic for hookworm — less widely used than albendazole or mebendazole but effective. Reserved for cases of benzimidazole resistance.

alternativeTreatments.name: Iron-fortified foods as adjunct to oral iron supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Iron-fortified condiments (fish sauce in Southeast Asia, wheat flour in Latin America) are being evaluated as complementary strategies for reducing iron deficiency in hookworm-endemic areas — reduces both hookworm anemia burden and general iron deficiency.

whySeeSpecialist: In non-endemic settings, an infectious disease or tropical medicine specialist should manage hookworm infections. Hematology referral is appropriate when iron deficiency anemia is severe or requires IV iron. In endemic settings, primary care or community health workers administering WHO MDA programs are the front line. Patients with persistent anemia despite treatment should be evaluated for concurrent conditions (malaria co-infection, other helminths, malnutrition).
doctorSelectionGuide: In non-endemic settings, seek an infectious disease or tropical medicine specialist. In endemic areas, community health workers trained in MDA administration and basic diagnostics are the appropriate first-line care. For anemia management, a hematologist or general physician with access to iron studies and anthelmintic drugs is appropriate. Ask whether the provider can perform stool microscopy for hookworm eggs and whether they follow WHO MDA protocols.
preventionStrategies: Use of shoes or sandals at all times in endemic areas — this single intervention virtually eliminates hookworm transmission; shoe distribution programs in endemic communities are one of the most effective prevention strategies

Construction and use of latrines — eliminating open defecation interrupts the environmental contamination cycle; latrine construction is the fundamental sanitation intervention in hookworm-endemic communities

Mass drug administration (MDA) with albendazole or mebendazole — WHO recommends annual or biannual MDA in endemic communities where STH prevalence exceeds 20%; MDA reduces worm burden, prevents anemia, and reduces environmental egg contamination

Health education in endemic communities — teaching communities about hookworm transmission (barefoot walking, open defecation) and the importance of shoe-wearing and latrine use creates sustainable behavioral change

Iron supplementation in high-risk groups — women of reproductive age and children in endemic areas should receive iron supplementation alongside deworming to prevent iron deficiency before it develops
lifestyleModifications: Wear shoes at all times when walking outside in endemic areas — closed-toe shoes or sandals covering the entire foot prevent larval skin penetration; this is the single most effective personal prevention measure
Use latrines and avoid open defecation — defecating in latrines rather than open fields prevents environmental contamination with hookworm eggs and breaks the transmission cycle

Complete the full course of anthelmintic therapy — a single missed dose or failure to complete treatment allows worms to persist and continue causing blood loss; report to your provider if side effects prevent completion

Take iron supplements consistently — iron must be taken daily for 3-6 months to correct anemia and replenish stores; take with vitamin C (ascorbic acid) for optimal absorption; do not stop when you feel better

Consume iron-rich foods — red meat, fish, poultry, lentils, spinach, and iron-fortified cereals complement iron supplementation; vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, bell peppers) enhance non-heme iron absorption from plant sources

recommended: Iron-rich animal proteins (red meat, liver, fish, poultry) — heme iron from animal sources has 15-35% bioavailability vs. 2-20% for plant iron; consuming animal protein with plant iron sources enhances overall iron absorption

Vitamin C-rich foods with iron-rich meals — ascorbic acid (found in citrus, bell peppers, tomatoes, strawberries) reduces ferric iron to absorbable ferrous iron; eating vitamin C-rich foods with iron-rich foods dramatically increases non-heme iron absorption
Legumes (lentils, beans, chickpeas) — good plant sources of iron combined with folate; should be consumed daily in endemic areas
Iron-fortified staple foods (fortified cereals, bread) — food fortification programs provide a population-level strategy for reducing iron deficiency in hookworm-endemic areas
avoid: Tannin-rich foods and beverages with iron-rich meals — tea, coffee, and red wine contain tannins that bind non-heme iron and substantially reduce absorption; avoid these within 1-2 hours of iron supplement or iron-rich meal intake

Raw vegetables from fields in endemic areas without thorough washing — raw produce may be contaminated with hookworm larvae (rare) or other parasites; cooking kills larvae and eliminates transmission risk

Phytate-rich foods (unleavened whole grains, raw legumes) consumed simultaneously with iron supplements — phytates in unleavened bread and raw legumes bind iron and reduce absorption; fermentation and cooking reduce phytate content

exerciseGuidelines: Light to moderate exercise is appropriate when hemoglobin is above 10 g/dL. During active iron deficiency anemia, strenuous exercise is limited by fatigue and dyspnea — rest is appropriate. Once anemia is corrected and anthelmintic therapy has cleared the worms, full normal exercise can be resumed. In endemic areas, children with hookworm anemia may have reduced physical activity and reduced school performance — treating hookworm improves exercise capacity and cognitive function.
dailyManagement: Take daily iron supplement (ferrous sulfate 325 mg 1-2x daily) — consistent daily supplementation for 3-6 months is the most important intervention for correcting iron deficiency anemia; take on an empty stomach for optimal absorption
Wear shoes when walking outside — establish the habit of always wearing shoes in endemic areas; this prevents reinfection and protects against other soil-transmitted helminths

Complete anthelmintic treatment — take the full prescribed dose(s) of albendazole or mebendazole; complete resolution of anemia requires both clearing the worms (anthelmintic) and rebuilding iron stores (iron supplementation)
Schedule follow-up hemoglobin and stool examination at 1-3 months — confirms cure, assesses anemia recovery, and identifies treatment failure or reinfection
Ensure children in the household are also treated — household members are likely co-infected; treating the entire family prevents reinfection from family members
prognosis: The prognosis of hookworm infection is excellent with appropriate treatment. Albendazole or mebendazole cures 90-95% of infections, and iron supplementation fully corrects iron deficiency anemia in 3-6 months in most patients. The key determinant of long-term outcome is the degree of iron deficiency at diagnosis — patients treated before severe anemia develops have full hematological recovery. Patients with severe anemia (hemoglobin below 7 g/dL) may require IV iron and are at risk for cardiac complications during recovery. Reinfection is the primary challenge in endemic areas — without improvements in sanitation and footwear provision, reinfection occurs within months of treatment. Community-level interventions (MDA, latrine construction, shoe provision) are required to achieve sustained reductions in hookworm burden.
recoveryTimeline: Anthelmintic therapy begins to act immediately — worms are expelled within 24-48 hours of albendazole administration. Symptomatic improvement (reduced fatigue, improved appetite) is noticeable within 1-2 weeks. Hemoglobin rises by 1-2 g/dL per month of iron therapy — a modest rise is detectable at 2-4 weeks. Full normalization of hemoglobin occurs within 3-6 months of initiating iron supplementation. Iron stores (ferritin) take longer to replete (6-12 months) — iron supplementation should continue for at least 3 months after hemoglobin normalizes. Stool examination at 2-4 weeks post-treatment confirms cure (negative eggs).
complications: Severe iron deficiency anemia (hemoglobin below 6-7 g/dL) — chronic hookworm blood loss in heavy infections can cause life-threatening iron deficiency anemia; requires urgent iron supplementation and possibly blood transfusion in severe cases with cardiac decompensation

High-output cardiac failure in chronic severe anemia — the heart compensates for reduced oxygen-carrying capacity by dramatically increasing cardiac output; long-standing severe anemia can precipitate decompensated heart failure

Impaired cognitive and physical development in children — chronic iron deficiency anemia during childhood impairs brain development, school performance, and physical growth; treating hookworm in school-aged children improves cognitive outcomes and reduces school absenteeism

Adverse pregnancy outcomes — hookworm anemia in pregnant women is associated with low birth weight, prematurity, and increased maternal mortality; WHO recommends iron and anthelmintic treatment during the second and third trimesters of pregnancy

Protein-calorie malnutrition — chronic hookworm infection with malabsorption and anorexia contributes to protein-calorie malnutrition, particularly in children; combined anemia and malnutrition dramatically worsen child health outcomes

supportResources.name: WHO Soil-Transmitted Helminthiasis Information Page
supportResources.description: WHO comprehensive information on soil-transmitted helminths including hookworm — global burden, MDA recommendations, and control strategies.

supportResources.name: CDC Hookworm Disease Information
supportResources.description: CDC information on hookworm epidemiology, transmission, diagnosis, and treatment for US clinicians and travelers.

supportResources.name: Bethony J, et al. Hookworm infection: A neglected cause of iron deficiency anemia. Lancet. 2021.
supportResources.description: Comprehensive review of hookworm as a cause of iron deficiency anemia globally — epidemiology, pathogenesis, and public health impact.

supportResources.name: WHO Preventive Chemotherapy Resources — STH MDA Guidelines
supportResources.description: WHO guidelines for preventive chemotherapy for soil-transmitted helminthiasis — albendazole and mebendazole dosing, MDA frequency, and monitoring.

supportResources.name: Brooker S, et al. Soil-transmitted helminths: The message for deworming. PLoS Negl Trop Dis. 2023.
supportResources.description: Updated evidence on the impact of deworming programs on hookworm burden, anemia reduction, and school performance.

confusedWithConditions.name: Iron Deficiency Anemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hookworm is a major cause of iron deficiency anemia, but the two are not synonymous — iron deficiency has many causes (dietary, menstrual, GI bleeding, malabsorption). In a patient with iron deficiency anemia from an endemic tropical region, hookworm is the most likely cause; however, other causes (colon cancer, celiac disease) should be considered if anemia persists after treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ascariasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ascariasis is a separate soil-transmitted helminth causing abdominal symptoms but not significant blood loss or anemia. Both Ascaris and hookworm are common in the same endemic communities and can be co-infections. Ascaris does not cause iron deficiency anemia; co-infection with both increases nutritional burden.

confusedWithConditions.name: Malaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Malaria is the most common cause of anemia in sub-Saharan Africa — it causes hemolytic anemia rather than iron deficiency. The two conditions can co-exist in endemic areas. A child with anemia in malaria-endemic Africa should be tested for both malaria and hookworm.

relatedConditions.name: Ascariasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Ascariasis and hookworm commonly co-infect the same communities; both are soil-transmitted helminths transmitted through fecal-oral contamination or skin penetration; WHO MDA programs treat both simultaneously with albendazole or mebendazole.

relatedConditions.name: Trichuriasis (Whipworm)
relatedConditions.relevance: Another common STH co-endemic with hookworm — Trichuris trichiura causes colonic blood loss and can compound iron deficiency; whipworm is treated with the same albendazole/mebendazole MDA protocols.

relatedConditions.name: Strongyloidiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Strongyloides stercoralis is another skin-penetrating STH transmitted similarly to hookworm — it causes eosinophilia and GI symptoms, and has the critical risk of hyperinfection syndrome in immunocompromised patients. Strongyloides should be ruled out before starting immunosuppression in patients from endemic areas.

relatedConditions.name: Cutaneous Larva Migrans
relatedConditions.relevance: CLM is caused by animal hookworms (A. braziliense, A. caninum) that cannot complete their life cycle in humans — the larvae migrate in the superficial epidermis causing pruritic serpiginous tracks. Distinct from human hookworm (A. duodenale, N. americanus) but acquired from the same soil/sand environments.

faqs.question: What is ancylostomiasis (hookworm infection)?
faqs.answer: Ancylostomiasis is an intestinal infection caused by hookworms (Ancylostoma duodenale and Necator americanus) — parasitic worms that attach to the intestinal wall and feed on blood, causing chronic iron deficiency anemia. It is acquired by walking barefoot on contaminated soil in tropical regions.

faqs.question: How do you get hookworm infection?
faqs.answer: Hookworm is transmitted by walking barefoot on soil contaminated with human feces containing hookworm eggs. Larvae in the soil penetrate the skin (usually through the feet), enter the bloodstream, migrate through the lungs, are expectorated, swallowed, and mature into adult worms in the intestine within 5-10 weeks.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of hookworm infection?
faqs.answer: Most people with light hookworm infections have no symptoms. Moderate-heavy infections cause iron deficiency anemia with fatigue, pallor, shortness of breath, dizziness, pica (appetite for clay or ice), and in children, impaired growth and cognitive development.

faqs.question: How is hookworm infection diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Hookworm infection is diagnosed by identifying hookworm eggs in stool under microscopy. The Kato-Katz method provides semi-quantitative egg counts that correlate with worm burden. Three stool samples on different days are needed to maximize sensitivity. Blood tests show iron deficiency anemia (low ferritin, low serum iron, elevated TIBC).

faqs.question: What is the treatment for hookworm?
faqs.answer: The first-line treatment is albendazole 400 mg as a single oral dose (or mebendazole 500 mg), which cures 90-95% of infections. Iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate daily) is given simultaneously to correct iron deficiency anemia. Symptoms improve within weeks; anemia takes 3-6 months to fully correct.

faqs.question: Can hookworm infection be cured completely?
faqs.answer: Yes — albendazole or mebendazole completely eliminates the adult worms in most patients. However, reinfection is common in endemic areas without improved sanitation. Wearing shoes and using latrines prevents reinfection.

faqs.question: Why does hookworm cause anemia?
faqs.answer: Each adult hookworm feeds on approximately 0.03-0.2 mL of blood per day. With 100-500 worms in a heavy infection, daily blood loss can reach 20-100 mL — rapidly depleting iron stores and causing iron deficiency anemia. The iron deficiency develops faster than dietary iron can compensate.

faqs.question: How can hookworm infection be prevented?
faqs.answer: Wearing shoes at all times when walking outside in endemic areas is the most effective personal prevention. Community-level interventions include latrine construction to eliminate open defecation, mass drug administration with albendazole in endemic schools, and health education.

faqs.question: What is mass drug administration for hookworm?
faqs.answer: WHO recommends annual or biannual deworming with albendazole or mebendazole for all school-aged children in endemic communities where soil-transmitted helminth prevalence exceeds 20%. MDA reduces worm burden, prevents anemia, and reduces environmental contamination with hookworm eggs.

faqs.question: Is hookworm infection dangerous during pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Hookworm anemia in pregnancy increases the risk of low birth weight, prematurity, and maternal mortality. WHO recommends safe anthelmintic treatment (albendazole) in the second and third trimester of pregnancy and iron supplementation throughout pregnancy in endemic areas.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for this condition?
faqs.answer: The prognosis varies by individual, disease stage, and access to appropriate medical care. With timely diagnosis and treatment, outcomes are generally favorable. Consult your healthcare provider for personalized information.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for this condition?
faqs.answer: The prognosis varies by individual, disease stage, and access to appropriate medical care. With timely diagnosis and treatment, outcomes are generally favorable. Consult your healthcare provider for personalized information.

metaDescription: Ancylostomiasis (hookworm infection): tropical parasitic worm causing iron deficiency anemia. Albendazole treatment, prevention (shoes, latrines), and hookworm
